content:
# Galatians Chapter 5 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

---

### v.1 — "For the freedom Christos freed us" vs. "For freedom Christ has set us free" vs. "Christ has set us free to live a free life"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "For the freedom Christos freed us; stand therefore" | Cognate construction preserved; literal |
| LOGOS | "For freedom Christ has set us free; stand firm therefore" | "Set us free" — standard; "stand firm" |
| PNEUMA | "Christ has set us free to live a free life. So take your stand! Never again let anyone put a harness of slavery on you" | "Free life" added; "harness" for "yoke"; two sentences |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The cognate construction (eleutheria...ēleutherōsen) is important but LOGOS preserves its sense. PNEUMA adds "to live a free life" (not in Greek), changes "yoke" to "harness," and adds "Never again let anyone" — expanding the imperative.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds and restructures.

---

### v.2 — "if you are circumcised" vs. "if you accept circumcision" vs. "if you are counting on circumcision to make you right with God"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "if you are circumcised" | ean peritemnēsthe — present subjunctive |
| LOGOS | "if you accept circumcision" | "Accept" — volitional aspect |
| PNEUMA | "if you are counting on circumcision to make you right with God" | Adds purpose — "to make you right with God" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "Accept circumcision" captures the volitional aspect — these are Gentiles choosing to be circumcised. PNEUMA adds the purpose clause "to make you right with God" — Paul's point, but not explicitly stated in this verse.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds implied purpose.

---

### v.4 — "severed from Christos" vs. "severed from Christ" vs. "cut off from Christ"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "You have been severed from Christos" | katērgēthēte — "rendered inoperative/severed" |
| LOGOS | "You are severed from Christ" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "you have been cut off from Christ! You have fallen away from God's grace" | "Cut off"; adds "God's" to "grace" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "Severed" better captures katargeō (which means "render inoperative") than PNEUMA's "cut off" (which is more physical). PNEUMA also adds "God's" to "grace" — the Greek says simply "the grace" (tēs charitos).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. katargeō — "severed" vs. "cut off."

---

### v.6 — "faith working through love" vs. "faith expressing itself through love"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "faith working through love" | pistis di' agapēs energoumenē — middle participle |
| LOGOS | "faith working through love" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "faith expressing itself through love" | "Expressing itself" — reflexive rendering |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. energoumenē (middle voice of energeō) means "working/being active." LOGOS and EMET agree on "working." PNEUMA's "expressing itself" is a valid rendering of the middle voice but shifts from active work to self-expression — a subtle difference.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor — "working" vs. "expressing itself."

---

### v.7 — "Who hindered you" vs. "Who cut in on you" vs. "Who has held you back"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Who hindered you from obeying [the] truth?" | enekopsen — "cut in / hinder" |
| LOGOS | "Who cut in on you to keep you from obeying the truth?" | "Cut in on" — athletic metaphor |
| PNEUMA | "Who has held you back from following the truth?" | "Held you back"; "following" for "obeying" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. enkoptō literally means "cut into" — from the athletic arena, where a runner cuts in front of another. LOGOS preserves the athletic metaphor that continues from "running well." PNEUMA loses the cutting imagery with "held you back."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Athletic metaphor — "cut in" vs. "held back."

---

### v.8 — "The persuasion is not from the one calling you" vs. expanded identification

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The persuasion [is] not from the [one] calling you" | hē peismonē ouk ek tou kalountos hymas |
| LOGOS | "This persuasion is not from him who calls you" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "It certainly isn't God, for he is the one who called you" | Identifies "the one calling" as God; adds "certainly" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Paul says "the one calling you" without naming God explicitly. PNEUMA names God and restructures with "certainly" — resolving the ambiguity and adding emphasis.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA identifies what Paul leaves implicit.

---

### v.9 — "leaven" vs. "yeast" and proverbial handling

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "A little leaven leavens the whole lump" | zymē — "leaven"; proverb preserved |
| LOGOS | "A little leaven leavens the whole lump" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "This false teaching is like a little yeast that spreads through the whole batch of dough!" | "False teaching" as subject; "yeast" for "leaven"; simile structure |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Paul quotes a proverb without explaining it. PNEUMA identifies the leaven as "this false teaching" — Paul's point, but Paul lets the proverb speak for itself. PNEUMA also changes the proverb into a simile ("is like") rather than preserving its proverbial form.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA interprets the proverb rather than preserving it.

---

### v.11 — "the stumbling-block of the cross" vs. "the offense of the cross" vs. expanded explanation

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the stumbling-block of the cross has been abolished" | to skandalon tou staurou — "the stumbling-block" |
| LOGOS | "the offense of the cross has been removed" | "Offense" — standard rendering of skandalon |
| PNEUMA | "If I were no longer preaching salvation through the cross of Christ, no one would be offended" | Full restructuring; adds "salvation through" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. skandalon means "stumbling-block/offense." LOGOS renders the meaning concisely. PNEUMA restructures into a conditional clause and adds "salvation through" — expanding Paul's compressed statement.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA restructures and adds content.

---

### v.12 — "cut themselves off" vs. "emasculate themselves" vs. "mutilate themselves"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "would even cut themselves off" | apokopsontai — "cut off" |
| LOGOS | "would emasculate themselves" | "Emasculate" — castration reference explicit |
| PNEUMA | "who want to mutilate you by circumcision would mutilate themselves" | "Mutilate" — adds "you by circumcision" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. apokoptō means "cut off" and in this context refers to castration — Paul's shocking escalation. "Emasculate" makes the reference clear. PNEUMA's "mutilate" is adequate but adds "you by circumcision" — not in the Greek. EMET's "cut themselves off" preserves the ambiguity (cut off from community? castrate?).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. apokoptō — castration reference.

---

### v.13 — "opportunity for the flesh" vs. "satisfy your sinful nature"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "do not [use] the freedom as an opportunity for the flesh" | aphormēn tē sarki — "a base of operations for the flesh" |
| LOGOS | "do not use your freedom as an opportunity for the flesh" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "don't use your freedom to satisfy your sinful nature" | "Satisfy your sinful nature" for "opportunity for the flesh" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. aphormē means "starting point/base of operations" — a military term. "Opportunity" preserves this. PNEUMA renders sarx as "sinful nature" — an interpretive choice that imports a particular anthropology. Paul's sarx ("flesh") is broader than "sinful nature."

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. sarx as "flesh" vs. "sinful nature." This rendering choice affects the entire flesh-Spirit section (vv.13-26). "Flesh" in Paul is the whole human being oriented away from God — not just a "sinful nature" within the person. "Sinful nature" implies an inner component that can be isolated, which is closer to Greek dualism than Paul's Jewish anthropology.

---

### v.16-17 — "flesh" vs. "sinful nature" throughout

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "desire of [the] flesh...the flesh desires against the Spirit" | sarx throughout |
| LOGOS | "desires of the flesh...the desires of the flesh are against the Spirit" | Same — "flesh" |
| PNEUMA | "selfish desires of your sinful nature...the sinful nature wants to do evil" | "Sinful nature" for sarx throughout |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Same issue as v.13 — PNEUMA consistently renders sarx as "sinful nature" throughout the chapter. Additionally, PNEUMA adds "wants to do evil" — Paul says the flesh "desires against" (epithymei kata) the Spirit, not that it "wants to do evil."

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. Systematic rendering of sarx as "sinful nature" — affects vv.13, 16, 17, 19, 24. This is one of the most consequential translation choices in the chapter.

---

### v.19-21 — Vice list: individual term differences

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "enmities, strife, jealousy, outbursts of anger, selfish ambitions, dissensions, factions, envyings, drunkennesses, revelries" | Plural forms; "factions" for haireseis |
| LOGOS | "enmity, strife, jealousy, fits of anger, rivalries, dissensions, divisions, envy, drunkenness, orgies" | Singular forms; "divisions" for haireseis |
| PNEUMA | "hostility, quarreling, jealousy, outbursts of anger, selfish ambition, dissension, division, envy, drunkenness, wild parties" | "Wild parties" for kōmoi; "hostility" for echthra |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Minor differences across the vice list. Most notable: EMET preserves Greek plurals (drunkennesses, revelries); LOGOS uses English convention (singular); PNEUMA's "wild parties" for kōmoi (orgies/carousing) is lighter than the Greek. haireseis ("factions/divisions") is the root of "heresies" but in Paul means party spirit.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor — vice list vocabulary.

---

### v.24 — "have crucified the flesh with the passions and the desires" — structural variation

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "have crucified the flesh with the passions and the desires" | tēn sarka syn tois pathēmasin kai tais epithymiais |
| LOGOS | "have crucified the flesh with its passions and desires" | "Its" — possessive clarity |
| PNEUMA | "have nailed the passions and desires of their sinful nature to his cross and crucified them there" | Adds "nailed...to his cross"; "sinful nature" again |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "nailed...to his cross and crucified them there" — dramatically expanding the metaphor. Paul says simply "crucified the flesh with its passions and desires" — a single verb, not a two-step process. PNEUMA also continues the "sinful nature" rendering.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA dramatizes and expands the metaphor.

---

### v.25 — "walk by the Spirit" vs. "keep in step with the Spirit" vs. "follow the Spirit's leading"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "let us also walk by [the] Spirit" | stoichōmen — "walk in line / keep in step" |
| LOGOS | "let us also keep in step with the Spirit" | "Keep in step" — stoicheō distinct from peripateō |
| PNEUMA | "let us follow the Spirit's leading in every part of our lives" | "Follow...leading"; adds "in every part of our lives" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Paul uses two different verbs for "walk" in vv.16 and 25: peripateō (v.16, "walk around/conduct oneself") and stoicheō (v.25, "walk in line/keep in step"). LOGOS distinguishes them. PNEUMA's "follow the Spirit's leading" loses the walking imagery and adds "in every part of our lives."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Two distinct walking verbs — peripateō vs. stoicheō.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA sarx as "sinful nature" | 5 | Systematic rendering throughout vv.13-24 |
| PNEUMA interpretive additions | 4 | "False teaching" (v.9), "salvation through" (v.11), "to his cross" (v.24), "every part" (v.25) |
| PNEUMA structural changes | 3 | Restructures proverb (v.9), conditional (v.11), metaphor (v.24) |
| Athletic/military metaphor | 2 | "Cut in" vs. "held back" (v.7); aphormē as "opportunity" vs. "satisfy" (v.13) |
| apokoptō rendering | 1 | Castration reference — "emasculate" vs. "mutilate" vs. "cut off" |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 5's most consequential disagreement is PNEUMA's systematic rendering of sarx as "sinful nature" rather than "flesh." This affects at least five verses and imports a particular anthropological framework — an internal "sinful nature" component — that differs from Paul's more holistic understanding of sarx as the whole person oriented away from God. The sarx/"sinful nature" choice has shaped centuries of popular Christian theology, and the difference matters: "flesh" implies a mode of existence; "sinful nature" implies a defective component within the self.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
